title: The False Alibi
description: Two wrong publications and a suspicious R-aspect article. The naive plan wastes a step — but one well-chosen master mix removes two targets at once and reveals why the article is wrong.

hints.text: Three targets: Fern's publication (claims Ppn), Mushroom's publication (claims Nnp), and Art-1 (R aspect, partially wrong). The naive plan is three separate apprentice reveals — but the minimum is two steps. Look for a master mix that handles two targets at once.

Consider mixing Fern (1) + Mandrake (5). True result: R−.

Fern's publication claims Ppn. Predicted mix(Ppn + Nnp) = neutral ≠ R−, so there is a conflict on Fern's publication. Mandrake has no wrong publication, so blame is unambiguous → Fern's publication removed.

But there is more: the mix result R− also tells you that Fern and Mandrake share R−. Since Fern's true R is − (confirmed by clues), the result confirms Mandrake's R is also −. Art-1 claims Mandrake has R+, which this directly contradicts → Art-1 removed.

Two targets gone in step 1. Only Mushroom's publication remains.

Optimal 2-step plan:
① Master: mix Fern + Mandrake (1 + 5) → true result R−.
   • Removes Fern's publication: mix(claimed Ppn + true Nnp) = neutral ≠ R−, conflict is unambiguous (only Fern has a wrong pub in this pair).
   • Simultaneously: result R− with Fern known as R−S proves Mandrake is R−L. Art-1 claims Mandrake (5) is R+, so the contradiction removes Art-1.
② Apprentice: reveal R+ on Mushroom (3). Mushroom's true alchemical pNn is R+; the publication claims Nnp which is R−. Contradiction → Mushroom's publication removed.

Bonus — Q2 (conflict only, fixed ingredient = Fern):
Mix Fern + Mushroom (1 + 3) → true result B−. Both Fern's publication (claims Ppn) and Mushroom's publication (claims Nnp) are in conflict. Since both are wrong, neither can be unambiguously blamed → neither is removed. Pure conflict only.
